data.title: Tel Aviv stocks rebound as markets gauge effects of Iran-Israel conflict
data.description:
Israeli equities closed higher on Sunday despite an escalation in hostilities between Israel and Iran, with investors appearing to shake off early concerns about the regional conflict. The Tel Aviv Stock Exchange’s benchmark TA-35 index ended the day up 1%, recovering from morning losses that had approached 2%.

Sunday marked the first trading session since a dramatic exchange of missile strikes between the two nations began on Friday. Market sentiment wavered early in the day, weighed down by fresh violence and a steep selloff on Wall Street late last week. But by the close, equities had broadly regained ground.

Ronen Menachem, chief markets economist at Mizrahi Tefahot Bank, was quoted by Reuters as saying that investor reactions were initially driven by geopolitical shock and global market weakness. Going forward, investor sentiment is likely to be influenced by how the conflict unfolds, particularly its scope, duration and how the United States chooses to respond, he said.

While local government bonds saw slight gains, trading in the Israeli shekel was paused for the day. The currency had weakened on Friday, falling from 3.5 to 3.61 per dollar.

Iran retaliates to Israel’s strikes

Israel reported that its military had launched targeted strikes on Iranian facilities tied to nuclear development and missile production over the weekend. Iran retaliated with a barrage of rockets, marking the second consecutive day of direct conflict. At least 10 Israeli civilians were killed overnight, including three children, and more than 140 were injured.

In response to the attacks, the Home Front Command implemented emergency measures, including a ban on public gatherings, remote learning for schools and a shift to remote work for non-essential employees. Despite this, the Bank of Israel announced that Sunday would proceed as a standard business day. Major bank branches remained open and central authorities emphasized that financial systems and payment infrastructure were functioning normally.

“The Bank has all necessary tools to support economic continuity,” the central bank said in a statement.

A senior Finance Ministry official told Reuters that while the conflict may result in increased spending, Israel enters this period from a position of financial strength, with deep liquidity in domestic markets. The official also said the heightened military activity could prompt credit rating agencies to reassess Israel’s standing, though the shekel’s performance suggests there is no sense of crisis for now.

Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich said preparations had been underway for weeks and that the government was ready to act as needed, Reuters reported.

data.title: Trump reports over $57M in crypto earnings, along with income from resorts
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump disclosed $57.7 million in income from cryptocurrency token sales linked to a firm he and his sons co-founded, according to federal financial disclosure documents cited by multiple news outlets. The crypto earnings make up part of the more than $600 million in income reported by the president.

The filing, published Friday by the Office of Government Ethics, offers a comprehensive snapshot of Trump’s finances, revealing earnings across his business empire. His holdings include everything from real estate and hospitality ventures to digital assets and crypto projects.

The crypto windfall stems from World Liberty Financial, a blockchain-based venture launched ahead of the 2024 election. Trump and his sons Donald Jr., Eric and Barron are listed as co-founders on the company’s website.

Trump’s biggest source of private income came from Trump Endeavor 12 Llc, a Miami-based entity that owns several golf properties and a resort, which brought in $110 million. Meanwhile, his Mar-a-Lago estate generated more than $50 million in revenue last year.

The president, whose net worth is estimated at $4.8 billion by the Bloomberg Billionaires Index, reported 22 assets with individual values exceeding $50 million, including Mar-a-Lago, his golf resort in Turnberry, Scotland, and shares in World Liberty Financial and Trump Media & Technology Group, the parent company of Truth Social.

Because the disclosure form uses broad valuation ranges (with “over $50 million” being the highest), it doesn’t provide precise figures for calculating net worth. Trump Media is currently valued at approximately $2.2 billion.

Meme coin assets

A separate crypto-related company, Fight Fight Fight Llc, the operator behind Trump’s meme coin, launched in January 2025 and wasn't covered in the 2024 disclosure. However, in May, it hosted an exclusive dinner for top holders of the $TRUMP token, which saw its value spike by 56% after the event was announced.

Another digital asset stream, CIC Digital Llc, which licenses Trump’s image for NFTs, generated $1.1 million in income last year and holds an Ethereum wallet valued at more than $1 million.

The 234-page filing also catalogues Trump's portfolio of trademarks across countries such as China, Taiwan, South Korea and Venezuela. It includes personal investments and holdings of First Lady Melania Trump.

Trump reported 11 unresolved debts, including two related to civil judgments awarded to author E. Jean Carroll and one stemming from his felony conviction in a criminal fraud case. These obligations are on hold while appeals are pending. Legal bills tied to his various lawsuits were not listed, as they have largely been covered by his Save America PAC.

The disclosure also reveals Trump holds seven real estate loans, including mortgages of more than $50 million each on Trump Tower, Trump National Doral and 40 Wall Street. He also reported at least $15,000 in credit card debt on his American Express account.

Separately, Vice President J.D. Vance disclosed that his household, including wife Usha Vance, held assets worth at least $6.5 million, Bloomberg News reported.

data.title: Notable healthcare headlines for the week: Novo Nordisk, Thermo Fisher and UnitedHealth in focus
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages plummeted on Friday after Israel and Iran traded missile strikes and stoked concerns of an all-out war in the Middle East.

Iran retaliated on Friday with its own projectiles after Israel’s attack on some of Iran’s nuclear sites and military targets on late Thursday.

Week-to-date, the S&P (SP500) lost 0.4%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was down 0.6%, and the Dow (DJI) declined 1.3%.

However, the S&P 500 Health Care Index Sector (NYSEARCA:XLV) gained nearly 2% during the week.

The top S&P 500 healthcare gainers and losers for the last week are as follows:

Top Gainers:

·       Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) +7.30%

·       Eli Lilly (LLY) +6.43%

·       CVS Health (CVS) +6.10%

·       Pfizer (PFE) +5.10%

·       Cardinal Health (CAH) +4.33%

Top Losers:

·       Universal Health Services (UHS) -9.67%

·       Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) -8.06%

·       DexCom (DXCM) -5.40%

·       The Cooper Companies (COO) -3.68%

·       Zoetis (ZTS) -3.43%

Here are some of the important healthcare stories from this week:

Novo Nordisk rises on report activist Parvus building stake

Novo Nordisk (NVO) shares hit their highest level in over two months following a Financial Times report that activist hedge fund Parvus Asset Management is building a stake in the Danish drugmaker. The stock rose as much as 2.5% in early trading in Copenhagen. Novo Nordisk told Seeking Alpha in an email that it does not have anything to “share on this topic.” The report comes as the pharma giant faces growing challenges in maintaining its dominance in the obesity drug market.

In other news of note, Novo Nordisk (NVO) has initiated a new Phase 3 trial for its next-gen weight loss therapy CagriSema after the Danish drugmaker's REDEFINE 1 Phase 3 trial for the treatment disappointed Wall Street late last year.

Additionally, Nvidia (NVDA) announced a collaboration with Novo Nordisk (NVO) on Wednesday to speed up the Danish pharma giant's efforts in drug discovery using artificial intelligence.

Thermo Fisher said to weigh sale of some diagnostic assets for $4B

Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) is looking to sell some of its lower-growth diagnostic assets for around $4B, the Financial Times reported, citing three people familiar with the matter.

The company's advisers approached private equity firms in recent weeks to gauge interest in a possible deal. However, there is no guarantee that a deal will materialize.

The diagnostics business, which includes Thermo Fisher's (TMO) microbiology unit that makes infectious disease testing solutions, generates over $1B in annual sales.

UnitedHealth weighs $1B deal for Banmedica in bid to exit Latin America - report

UnitedHealth (UNH) is considering four non-binding bids for its Banmedica unit for about $1B, two people with direct knowledge of the matter told Reuters, as the largest U.S. health insurer doubles down on efforts to exit Latin America.

The company is seeking $1B for Banmedica, which operates in Colombia and Chile, and expects to fix a deadline for binding offers next month. It'd acquired Banmedica for $2.8B in 2018.

UnitedHealth (UNH) reportedly received bids from U.S. private equity firm Acon Investments, Latin America-focused asset manager Patria Investments (PAX), Texas-based non-profit Christus Health, and Latin American healthcare firm Auna (AUNA).

While UnitedHealth (UNH) has been trying to exit Latin America since 2022 amid mounting losses, efforts have ramped up in recent months in the wake of its CEO's abrupt exit and an ongoing criminal probe.

BioNTech to buy German rival CureVac for $1.25B in all-stock deal

BioNTech (BNTX) has agreed to acquire its German rival CureVac (CVAC) in an all-stock transaction worth nearly $1.25B, the companies announced Thursday. Shares of CureVac (CVAC) jumped ~26% in reaction to the announcement, while BioNTech (BNTX) ADRs traded flat.

Per the terms of the deal, each CureVac (CVAC) share will be exchanged for approx. $5.46 in BioNTech American Depositary Shares (“ADSs”), indicating a premium of 55% to the former’s volume-weighted average price over the past three months.

CVAC shareholders are expected to own 4%–6% of BioNTech (BNTX) after the transaction, which gives the company an implied equity value of ~$1.25B.

Drugmakers complain clarity lacking from administration on lowering drug prices

Pharmaceutical executives say that the Trump administration has not provided enough details as to how it will achieve the lowered drug prices promised in an Executive Order issued in May.

That order implements a program known as "Most Favored Nation" that ties the prices of prescription medicines in the U.S. to the lowest prices found abroad.

data.title: Top global stories this week: Novo Nordisk, Xpeng and Tencent among notable names
data.description:
Most major stock indexes across the globe ended the week lower, following US-China trade talks and renewed geopolitical concerns.

U.S. President Donald Trump announced a trade framework with China, focusing on rare earth supplies and Chinese student access to U.S. universities. However, the move failed to  boost market sentiment. Trump announced plans to send letters to key trading partners within the next one to two weeks, outlining unilateral tariff rates.

Meanwhile, a fresh wave of conflict rattled global markets after Israel launched strikes targeting Iranian nuclear and military facilities, fuelling a sharp rally in oil prices and sending investors flocking to energy names.

At the end of the week, the S&P 500 (SP500) lost 0.4%, the Dow Jones (DJI) was down 1.1%, and the Nasdaq 100 (COMP:IND) fell 0.8%.

Across the Atlantic, the European equities (STOXX) ended the week lower by 1.7% as investors traded U.S.-China trade talks cautiously.

European officials, however, are less hopeful about their own trade negotiations with the US, cautioning that talks may stretch past the July 9 deadline and that only a preliminary agreement might be reached by that time.

On the data front, Euro Area recorded trade surplus of €9.9B in April. UK's economy contracted as UK's trade deficit widens in April. Industrial production in the UK declined by 0.6% M/M in April.

In the week, Germany (DAX:IND) and France (CAC:IND) equities dropped 2.9% and 1.4% respectively, while London’s FTSE 100 (UKX) ticked up 0.1%.

Corporate news from Europe this week:

  • Novo Nordisk (NVO) shares hit their highest level in over two months following a Financial Times report that activist hedge fund Parvus Asset Management is building a stake in the Danish drugmaker.
  • Qualcomm (QCOM) is acquiring British semiconductor company Alphawave IP (OTCPK:AWEVF) at an implied enterprise value of about $2.4B.
  •  Inditex (OTCPK:IDEXY) reported weaker-than-expected quarterly sales and noted a slow start to the summer season, attributing the downturn to economic headwinds.

The Asia-Pacific markets ended the week lower amid U.S.-China trade talks and a slew of regional economic data. Trump’s intention to outline unilateral tariff rates in the upcoming weeks kept investor sentiment at bay.

As per the terms of the latest trade deal between the two economic superpowers, China will provide any rare earths required by the U.S., while Chinese students will continue to have access to American universities. The deal also set the final U.S. tariff rate at 55% on Chinese imports, while China gets a 10% levy on U.S. goods.

Chinese markets (SHCOMP) fell 0.4% in the last week. China’s consumer prices fell for a fourth straight month and producer prices saw their steepest drop in nearly two years. Separately, the latest trade data revealed a decline in imports, driven by weak consumption. 

In the week, Japan markets (NKY:IND) dropped 0.8%.

Business sentiment continued to weaken in the second quarter, reflecting rising uncertainty over US trade policy and its impact on the Japan’s export-dependent economy. The country's GDP in Q1 2025 was flat, a better performance than the initial 0.2% contraction estimate, though significantly slower than Q4's 0.6% growth.

Major Asian corporate news: 

  • Chinese electric vehicle maker XPeng (XPEV) is developing advanced chips for Volkswagen (OTCPK:VLKAF) (OTCPK:VWAGY) cars, according to a report. The Turing process chips are expected to enhance computing capabilities in future Volkswagen models.
  •   Tencent Holdings (OTCPK:TCEHY) (OTCPK:TCTZF) is exploring a potential acquisition of the gaming company Nexon (OTCPK:NEXOF) to strengthen its position in the industry.

ETFs to track Europe: ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

ETFs to track Asia & Oceania: (NYSEARCA:EWJ), (NYSE:JEQ), (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:EWH), (NYSEARCA:DXJ), (NYSE:CAF), (NYSEARCA:FXY), (NYSEARCA:YINN), (NYSEARCA:YANG), (NASDAQ:CXSE), (NYSEARCA:KWEB)

data.title: Boeing trims jet demand outlook amid softer economic growth
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) expects global airlines will require around 43,600 new planes in the next two decades, with rising demand from China and Southeast Asia helping to drive expansion as more people gain access to air travel. The revised projection is slightly below last year’s estimate of 43,975 jets, reflecting a more cautious view of future economic growth.

Despite ongoing geopolitical tensions including trade disputes and tariffs that began under U.S. President Donald Trump, the aviation sector has historically proven capable of weathering global disruptions.

Darren Hulst, Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) vice president of commercial marketing, said at a June 10 press briefing that global air travel has shown remarkable durability, even rebounding from crises such as the Covid-19 pandemic. In the past quarter-century, passenger traffic has tripled while the size of the global aircraft fleet has doubled.

Hulst described the commercial aviation market as both resilient and fundamentally growth-oriented, despite near-term challenges.

Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) updated forecast sees the total number of commercial jets in operation reaching 49,600 by 2044, a figure that aligns closely with the outlook released by European rival Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY). Boeing  (BA) also projects that airlines in developing economies will operate over half the world’s aircraft by then, up from just under 40% today.

Importance of single-aisle aircraft

The company anticipates single-aisle aircraft will play an increasingly dominant role, making up 72% of the global fleet by 2044, compared with 66% currently.

This reflects the sustained popularity of narrowbody models such as Boeing’s (BA) 737 Max and Airbus’s (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) A320neo series, aircraft that remain difficult for airlines to acquire due to lingering supply constraints.

The surge in post-pandemic travel demand has outpaced production, leaving both Boeing (BA) and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) behind schedule. Hulst said the two manufacturers have collectively delivered about 1,500 fewer jets than originally planned, a gap that is likely to widen unless they ramp up production significantly.

Meeting future demand, he said, will require both companies to not only return to pre-Covid output levels, but also to exceed them by the latter part of the decade.

data.title: What are stablecoins?
data.description:
The word "stablecoin" has become the talk of town after Circle Internet's (NYSE:CRCL) blockbuster market debut on the New York Stock Exchange last week.

Class A shares of Circle (NYSE:CRCL), a stablecoin issuer, have surged a staggering +330.8% from their initial public offering price of $31, giving the company a market cap north of $27B. 

In the wake of Circle's (NYSE:CRCL) splashy debut, it appears that some of the biggest U.S. companies also want to get in on the act. A Wall Street Journal report this week said retail giants Walmart (WMT) and Amazon (AMZN), along with online travel agency Expedia (EXPE), have explored issuing their own stablecoins. 

So, What Are Stablecoins? 

Stablecoins are a type of digital token or cryptocurrency whose value is pegged one-for-one to an actual asset, such as fiat money or gold, in order to maintain a stable price. 

Stablecoins thus are different from other popular cryptocurrencies such as bitcoin (BTC-USD) whose values are highly volatile and fluctuate based on supply and demand.  

Some Stablecoin Stats

Stablecoins currently in circulation have a market cap exceeding $250B. Nearly 99% of these are pegged to the U.S. dollar.

Fiat-backed stablecoins comprise about 86% of the total circulating supply.

The largest stablecoin by value is Tether (USDT-USD), which has a market cap of $155.54B. In comparison, the world's largest cryptocurrency, bitcoin (BTC-USD), has a market cap of $2.08T.

(All stats according to CoinMarketCap).

Are Stablecoins Regulated?

The U.S. currently has no regulatory framework for stablecoins. 

However, Congress is currently working on it, with the GENIUS Act advancing in the Senate and the STABLE Act pending in the House.

See here the full text of the "Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins" or GENIUS Act.

See here the full text of the "Stablecoin Transparency and Accountability for a Better Ledger Economy" or STABLE Act.

Both acts are meant to establish a regime to regulate stablecoins. 

A Resilient Financial Ally

Cathie Wood's ARK Invest believes that stablecoins could become one of the U.S. government's most important strategic assets over the next five to ten years. 

"Tether, Circle, and the broader stablecoin industry could create one of the largest sources of demand for U.S. Treasuries in the coming years, potentially replacing China and Japan as the top holders by 2030," Lorenzo Valente, director of research of digital assets at ARK, said in a Seeking Alpha article last Monday. 

Aside from Tether (USDT-USD), here are some other stablecoins: (USDC-USD), (PYUSD-USD), (TUSD-USD), (USDP-USD), (BUSD-USD), and (GUSD-USD).

And here are some crypto-linked exchange-traded funds of interest: (NYSEARCA:BITQ), (NASDAQ:DAPP), (NASDAQ:BKCH), (NYSEARCA:BLOK), (NYSEARCA:CRPT), and (NYSEARCA:IBLC).

data.title: Caesars Entertainment most shorted S&P 500 consumer discretionary stock in May; Amazon least shorted
data.description:
Average short interest across the S&P 500 consumer discretionary stocks has increased to 2.71% of shares float in end-May, from 2.67% a month earlier.

Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) has been up 16.7% over the year, as compared to the 9.2% rise in the S&P500 index over the same period.

Stocks with the largest and least short positions

Caesars Entertainment (CZR) was the most shorted stock at 12.82% of shares float, followed by MGM Resorts International (MGM) with short interest of 10.27% and Pool (POOL) with 9.32%, respectively. 

Amazon.com (AMZN) was the least shorted stock, with 61.71M shares sold short or 0.65% of the shares float, followed by Home Depot (HD) and Garmin (GRMN) with short interest of 0.83% and 1.15%, respectively.

Industry Analysis

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

 

Other Specialty Retail was the most shorted industry within the sector, with 8.73% short interest as of the end of May, higher from 7.61% at the end of April.

Casinos and Gambling stocks took the second spot with 8.16% short interest as of May end, compared to 7.85% a month ago.

Bets against the Apparel, Accessories and Luxury Goods sector as a whole, however, have declined at the end of May, compared at April-end.

What Quantitative Measures say

Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system gives Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) a Hold rating, with 2.93 score.

The ETF has a strong liquidity grade of A+ and momentum grade of also C+. Notably, the ratings' system sees risk as a threat and assigns the ETF a grade of D in this category.

data.title: Analysts say Iran unlikely to shut Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's most vital oil arteries
data.description:
Israel's air strikes on Iran are unlikely to cause a major disruption to oil supply, and market analysts believe a full-scale shutoff of oil flows by closing the Strait of Hormuz is unlikely, perhaps even physically impossible.

Crude oil prices climbed 7% on Friday after Israel launched widescale strikes against Iran, targeting nuclear facilities and missile factories, raising the possibility of a wider war in the Middle East that could even lead to a worst-case scenario involving blockades in the Strait of Hormuz that could push prices above $100/bbl.

Some 20% of the world's total oil consumption passes through the strait, or ~20M bbl/day of oil, condensate and fuel.

Iran has threatened to shut the Hormuz waterway in the past but never acted upon it, with analysts saying closing it off would be a steep logistical challenge.

"Let's be real about the Strait of Hormuz. First of all, most of it is in Oman, not in Iran. Number two, it is wide enough that the Iranians cannot close it," Anas Alhajji, managing partner at Energy Outlook Advisors, told CNBC.

Alhajji said economics would be an equally important factor, since China is the top importer of Iranian oil, reportedly accounting for more than 75% of Iran's oil exports, and China is Iran's second largest overall trade partner.

Iran's friends would "suffer more than their enemies [from a shutdown]... so it's very hard to see that happening," Alhajji said.

"Iran's economy heavily relies on the free passage of goods and vessels through the seaway, as its oil exports are entirely sea-based. Finally, cutting off the Strait of Hormuz would be counterproductive to Iran's relationship" with China, J.P. Morgan analysts said.

"It is our understanding that it would be extremely difficult for Iran to close the strait for an extended period given the presence of the U.S. Fifth Fleet in Bahrain," RBC Capital Markets' head of global commodity strategy Helima Croft wrote.

Iran has been severely weakened during the past year, but with its back to the wall, the regime could turn to an attack on the strait as a last-ditch effort to remain in power.

In any event, heightened Middle East tensions come at a time of ample global oil supply, and rising production in the U.S., Brazil, Canada, and other non-OPEC countries has reduced the Middle East's global market share in recent years, which could help mitigate if not fully offset any supply disruption.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH)

data.title: Amazon to invest $13B in data center infrastructure in Australia over five years
data.description:
U.S. tech and e-commerce giant Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) on Saturday said it would invest AUD 20B ($12.97B) to expand data center infrastructure in Australia by 2029.

As part of the plans, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) said it would invest in three new solar farms in the Australian states of Victoria and Queensland, and would commit to buying a combined capacity of more than 170 MW across the farms.

"This is the largest investment our country has seen from a global technology provider, and is an exciting opportunity for Australia to build AI capability using secure, resilient infrastructure," Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said in a statement.

The Australian plans come just days after Amazon (AMZN) announced a combined $30B investment in data centers in the U.S. states of North Carolina and Pennsylvania.

data.title: Israel says striking military targets in Tehran, intercepting more Iranian missiles
data.description:
Israel's military on Saturday said the air force was carrying out strikes against military targets in Iran's capital Tehran. Meanwhile, Iran's state news agency said more missiles had been launched at Israel.

The conflict has been escalating since Israel late on Thursday hit Iran with a series of what it called "preemptive" strikes against nuclear sites and military targets. Iran responded with waves of missiles on Friday.

"While the IDF is operating to intercept missiles launched from Iran, the IAF is currently striking military targets in Tehran," Nadav Shoshani, international spokesperson for the Israel Defense Forces (IDF), said on X (formerly Twitter). 

Iran's state news agency, the Islamic Republic News Agency (IRNA), said that a new round of missile attacks had been started against Israel, adding that targets in the Israeli port city of Haifa had been hit.

Israel's military earlier on Saturday said it had eliminated nine of Iran's senior nuclear scientists and experts, a claim that was refuted by the spokesperson of Iran's atomic organization.

The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) has confirmed that Israeli airstrikes have hit Iran's Natanz and Esfahan nuclear sites.           

Oil prices (CL1:COM)(CO1:COM) have spiked in response to the conflict, U.S. stocks (SP500) have tumbled, and investors have flocked to traditional safe-haven asset gold (XAUUSD:CUR).  

Here are some Israel-focused exchange-traded funds of interest: (NYSEARCA:EIS), (BATS:IZRL), (NYSEARCA:ISRA), and (NYSEARCA:ITEQ).

And here are some ETFs that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).  

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
